content:
First, write 391=17·23.  We look for the smallest integer b for which there is some a<391 with

   lcm(a,b) > lcm(a,391)\,.

Observe that taking a to share exactly one prime‐factor with 391 makes lcm(a,391) as small as possible.  For example, if we take a=23 then

   lcm(23,391) = 391  (since gcd(23,391)=23).

We then want lcm(23,b)>391.  Now

   lcm(23,b) = 23·b / gcd(23,b)\,.

We need 23·b/gcd(23,b) >391 = 17·23
⇔ b/gcd(23,b) >17.

We check b in increasing order:

– For b=17,…,17: b/gcd(23,b)≤17 so no.  
– For b=18: gcd(23,18)=1, so b/gcd=18>17, and

     lcm(23,18)=23·18=414>391.

Hence (a,b)=(23,18) is a “pupusa,” and no smaller b works.  Therefore the minimum possible b is

\boxed{18}.

   lcm(23,391) = 391  (since gcd(23,391)=23).

We then want lcm(23,b)>391.  Now

   lcm(23,b) = 23·b / gcd(23,b)\,.

We need 23·b/gcd(23,b) >391 = 17·23
⇔ b/gcd(23,b) >17.

We check b in increasing order:

– For b=17,…,17: b/gcd(23,b)≤17 so no.  
– For b=18: gcd(23,18)=1, so b/gcd=18>17, and

     lcm(23,18)=23·18=414>391.

Hence (a,b)=(23,18) is a “pupusa,” and no smaller b works.  Therefore the minimum possible b is

\boxed{18}.
